1: šālaḥ + māšaḥ — šālaḥ (H7971, sent) and māšaḥ (H4886, anoint) together frame the covenant commission: Samuel acted as YHWH's agent, and Saul's kingship is entirely derived authority. The verse grounds the following command in that original commissioning — Saul's failure to obey is therefore a failure of covenant stewardship, not merely military insubordination.
2: pāqad (H6485) — "I remember" translates pāqadtî, but the verb's range includes "attend to, muster, call to account." YHWH is not merely recalling history; he is acting on a standing account. The same verb governs covenant blessing and curse (Exodus 20:5). ʾăšer śām lô derek bəʿălōtô mimmiṣrāyim — the relative clause grounds the command in the Exodus narrative: Amalek attacked from behind on the road (Exodus 17:8-16; Deuteronomy 25:17-19), and that covenantal score is now being settled.
3: ḥērem (H2763) — the root appears three times in vv3–9 as the pivotal term. ḥāramtāh means "devote to destruction" — the total consecration of enemy spoil to YHWH, leaving nothing for the victor. The ḥērem is not mere slaughter but a ritual act asserting YHWH's sole ownership of the victory. The list ("man and woman, child and nursing infant, ox and sheep, camel and donkey") is total; no exception is authorized. Saul's selective application of it is the crux of 1 Samuel 15.
4: Verse reports the muster at Telaim (ṭəlāʾîm). The numerical data (200,000 + 10,000 of Judah separately enumerated) reflects the administrative levy structure; Judah's separate count anticipates the coming split. No Hebrew lexical issue; the verse is narrative preparation.
5: wayāreb (H693, from ʾārab) — "set an ambush." The same root governs Amalek's original attack (Exodus 17:8 context; Deuteronomy 25:18 — Amalek "ambushed" Israel on the road). Saul uses Amalek's own tactical signature against them — the narrative symmetry is probably deliberate.
6: ḥesed (H2617) — "loyalty" in the MKT. Saul warns the Kenites away because they "showed loyalty (ḥesed) to all Israel when they came up from Egypt." The Kenites' covenant-loyalty is honored; this is the positive use of ḥesed — covenant-faithfulness that earns protection. The contrast with Saul's own covenant-unfaithfulness in what follows is implicit but pointed.
7: Geographic extent: "from Havilah to the approach of Shur, which lies east of Egypt." The breadth signals total victory — the same formula appears in Genesis 25:18 for Ishmael's territory. The ḥērem command has been executed on the land; only Agag and the best livestock remain.
8: wayyiḥyē with Agag — "He captured Agag alive" (ḥay, living). The specific mention of Agag's survival against the ḥērem command signals the violation immediately after the summary of general compliance. wayyaḥărem (he devoted to destruction) in the second half creates the contrast: the rest received the ḥērem; Agag did not.
9: ʾāḇāh (H14) — "refused" (lōʾ ʾāḇû, they would not consent/were unwilling). The verb denotes active refusal, not oversight. The translation notes flag ḥāmal (H2550, spared — "to commiserate, have compassion") applied to Agag and the livestock: the emotion that overrode the command was pity for valuable things. miśmān (fatlings) and ṭōḇ (good/best) are the categories spared. What was nimrāṣ (thin, despised) was devoted. The inversion — keeping the good, destroying the inferior — reveals that the ḥērem was treated as a waste-disposal mechanism, not a sacred act.
10: Formulaic prophetic reception: wayhî dəḇar YHWH ʾel šəmûʾēl lēʾmōr — "Then the word of the LORD came to Samuel, saying." The same formula governs the commissioning of prophets (1 Kings 17:2; Jeremiah 1:4). Its appearance here marks Samuel as the covenant mediator who will pronounce the formal verdict.
11:
nāḥam (H5162) — the theologically dense verb: "I regret (niḥamtî) that I made Saul king." The Niphal of nāḥam denotes being moved with sorrow to the point of changing one's course. It is used of YHWH relenting from judgment (Exodus 32:14; Jonah 3:10) and here of regretting a positive act. The same root appears in v29 and v35 with the opposite claim — that YHWH does not nāḥam. The paradox is not a contradiction but a theological calibration: YHWH genuinely responds to the moral reality of human choice (here, v11), yet his ultimate purposes and character are not subject to reversal (v29). Both truths must be held.

wayyiḥar lišmûʾēl — "Samuel was troubled/angry" (root ḥārāh, H2734, to burn/be kindled). The same word used for YHWH's anger in many texts; Samuel's reaction mirrors the divine response. wayyizʿaq ʾel YHWH kol hallaylāh — he "cried out to YHWH all night" — the intensity is extraordinary; prophetic intercession at its most urgent.
12: wayyāṣeḇ lô yād — "he set up a monument for himself" (yād literally "hand," but used for a stele or memorial inscription — cf. 2 Samuel 18:18, "Absalom's monument"). The self-erection of a monument before any accounting to Samuel or YHWH characterizes Saul's trajectory: victory is converted into personal honor rather than covenant obedience. Carmel-then-Gilgal: the movement from monument-building to the site of sacrifice shows Saul's priorities reversed.
13: bārûḵ ʾattāh laYHWH — Saul's greeting formula ("Blessed are you by the LORD") is a covenant-blessing invocation; its use in the context of disobedience is ironic. hăqîmōtî ʾet dəḇar YHWH — "I have carried out the LORD's command" — the perfect hăqîmōtî claims completed, accomplished action. Samuel's subsequent response demolishes this claim with auditory evidence.
14: qôl haṣṣōʾn — "the sound/voice of the sheep." The noun qôl (H6963) means both "sound" and "voice." Samuel's question activates the auditory register that will become central in v22: YHWH values šāmaʿ (hearing-obeying) above sacrifice, and here the sound of the livestock testifies against Saul's claim to have heard.
15: Saul deflects by identifying agency: "the people brought them." The shift from "I" (v13) to "the people" (v15) is the grammar of blame-shifting. The rationale offered — "to sacrifice to YHWH your God" — is liturgically framed but the possessive "your God" subtly distances Saul from covenant ownership. The best were spared; the rest destroyed: the same vocabulary as v9.
16: wayyōʾmer šəmûʾēl ʾel šāʾûl herpē — "Samuel said, Stop" (herpē, from rāpāh, H7503, to let go, desist). The command to be silent before hearing the prophetic word is itself a posture of submission. Saul's immediate "Speak" (daббēr) is formally compliant but the rest of the chapter shows he is not actually prepared to hear.
17: hălōʾ ʾim qāṭōn ʾattāh bəʿênêḵā — "when you were small in your own eyes." qāṭōn (H6996, small/young) coupled with bəʿênêḵā (in your eyes/estimation) — self-assessment of insignificance. Samuel's rebuke reactivates the original condition of Saul's appointment: he was chosen precisely when he did not presume on his own importance (1 Samuel 9:21). The contrast with the monument at Carmel (v12) is explicit.
18: derek (way/mission) + ḥāram — "sent you on a mission (derek) with an explicit command: Go and completely destroy (ḥāram)." The noun derek (H1870) means both road and mission/way of acting; the whole chapter is about whether Saul followed the derek he was given. derek hāʾămālēqî (the way of the Amalekites) — annihilation was the specific, bounded command.
19: lāmmāh lōʾ šāmaʿtā bəqôl YHWH — "Why did you not obey (šāmaʿ) the voice of YHWH?" The verb šāmaʿ (H8085) is both "hear" and "obey" — the two are not separable in Hebrew cognitive grammar. To hear YHWH's voice is to act on it; not acting proves not truly hearing. wattaʿaś hārāʿ bəʿênê YHWH — "you did what was evil in the eyes of YHWH" — the evaluative phrase is the narrative's own verdict formula (cf. Judges passim).
20: Saul's second self-defense uses šāmaʿtî (perfect: "I did obey/hear") but the verb is employed to mean only partial compliance. His claim to have "completely destroyed" the Amalekites (ḥāramtî ʾet ʾămālēq) is technically false: Agag lives. The speech demonstrates that Saul uses the covenant vocabulary without its substance.
21: wayyiqqaḥ hāʿām miššəlāl — "the people took from the plunder." Saul again attributes agency to "the people" rather than himself. rēʾšît haḥērem — "the choicest of what had been devoted" — the phrase uses rēʾšît (H7225, first/best/choicest), the same word for firstfruits offered to YHWH. The irony: the best of the ḥērem was redirected from its dedicated purpose to a competing sacrificial use.
22: šāmaʿ / šemen — Samuel's antithetical couplet: hăḥāpēṣ laYHWH bəʿōlôt ûzəḇāḥîm kišmōaʿ bəqôl YHWH — "Does YHWH delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as in hearing/obeying his voice?" The comparison uses kišmōaʿ (a Qal infinitive construct with prefix kaf: "as in hearing"). The rhetorical question expects "no." šāmaʿ is placed against zābaḥ (sacrifice); then the positive formulation: ṭôḇ šəmōaʿ mizzébaḥ (better to hear/obey than sacrifice) and ləhaqšîḇ méḥēleḇ ʾêlîm (to attend/hearken than the fat of rams). haqšêḇ (H7181, Hiphil of qāšaḇ) denotes attentive listening — the posture of the disciple who gives the teacher full hearing. The verse is not anti-sacrifice but rather establishes that sacrifice without obedience is a category error.
23: qesem / ʾāwen / tərāpîm — "rebellion is like the sin of divination (qesem); stubbornness is like wickedness (ʾāwen) and idolatry (tərāpîm)." Qesem (H7081) is a technical term for divination/witchcraft — specifically seeking guidance from spirits rather than YHWH. The comparison is structural: Saul's substitution of his own judgment for YHWH's command is functionally the same as consulting a medium. Tərāpîm (H8655) are household/fertility gods; the parallel equates stubbornness-over-YHWH's-command with idolatry. The key verb māʾastāh (Qal perfect, māʾas H3988, to reject/spurn) — "because you rejected the word of YHWH" — then governs the reversal: the same māʾas now applies to Saul: "he has rejected you as king."
24: ḥāṭāʾtî (perfect: "I have sinned") — this is Saul's only explicit confession; the motivation he gives is kî yārēʾtî ʾet hāʿām waʾešmaʿ bəqôlām — "because I feared the people and listened to their voice." The verb šāmaʿ bəqôl (heard/obeyed the voice) echoes v22: Saul šāmaʿ bəqôl hāʿām (obeyed the people's voice) but not šāmaʿ bəqôl YHWH (YHWH's voice). The substitution of voices — people over God — is the lexical center of the chapter.
25: śāʾ nāʾ lăḥaṭṭāʾtî — "forgive my sin" (nāśāʾ ḥēṭʾ, literally "carry/lift my sin" — the same idiom behind "bearing sins" in atonement theology; cf. Isaiah 53:12). Saul asks Samuel to be present at worship — the concern for public honor (kāḇōd) becomes explicit in v30.
26: lōʾ ʾāšûḇ ʿimmāḵ — "I will not return/go back with you." šûḇ (H7725, to return/turn) carries covenantal weight — the same root governs repentance and return to YHWH throughout the prophetic corpus. Samuel's refusal to "return" with Saul enacts the verdict: the covenant path has been closed. The grounding: kî māʾastāh ʾet dəḇar YHWH (same as v23) — the rejection is mutual in structure.
27: kannap məʿîlô — "the edge/hem of his robe." Kānāp (H3671) means both "wing" and "hem/edge" — the same word Ruth uses in 3:9 when she asks Boaz to "spread his garment" over her (invoking the goel). Here the tearing of the kānāp is an acted oracle. Samuel's məʿîl (H4598, robe/mantle) is the priestly robe of office; the tearing of what Saul grasps signals the tearing of what belongs to the anointed.
28: qāraʿ YHWH ʾet mamlᵉḵût yiśrāʾēl mēʿāliḵā hayyôm — "The LORD has torn (qāraʿ) the kingdom of Israel from you today." Qāraʿ (H7167, to tear) echoes the torn robe — the acted sign (v27) becomes the verbal verdict (v28). ûnətānāh lərēʿăḵā haṭṭôḇ mimmēḵā — "and given it to your neighbor who is better than you." Ṭôḇ mimmēḵā (better than you) uses the same ṭôḇ that appeared in vv9 and 21 for the livestock Saul preserved. The contrast is sharp: Saul valued ṭôḇ livestock over the command; now a ṭôḇ man replaces him.
29: nēṣaḥ yiśrāʾēl — "the Eternal One of Israel" (nēṣaḥ, H5331 — often "victory, glory, perpetuity"; the MKT's "Eternal One" captures the permanence meaning). lōʾ yəšaqqqer — "does not lie/deceive" (šāqar, H8266). wəlōʾ yinnāḥēm — "does not change his mind" (nāḥam again, same root as v11). The apparent contradiction with v11 (YHWH "regretted") is a theological claim, not an error: the nāḥam of v11 is genuine emotional response to a new moral state; the lōʾ yinnāḥēm of v29 asserts that YHWH's ultimate purposes — unlike human kings — are not subject to manipulation or reversal by human pressure. The verse is spoken precisely to Saul, who is about to appeal for public honor (v30).
30: kabbédnî nāʾ — "honor me, please" (kāḇēd, H3513, to be heavy/honored). Saul's concern shifts from sin to reputation: nəged ziqqənê ʿammî ûnəged yiśrāʾēl (before the elders of my people and before Israel). The word kāḇōd (honor/glory) governs Saul's final concern; what he fears is loss of face, not loss of covenant standing. Samuel complies — not because the verdict has changed but to prevent disorder.
31: The narrative is laconic: wayyiśtaḥû šāʾûl laYHWH — "Saul worshipped the LORD." The single verb records the act without endorsement; Samuel's presence does not restore the relationship. The reader is meant to note the irony: Saul performs the worship he claimed to be preserving the livestock for.
32: maʿădannōt — "hesitantly" (MT ʿdnt, variously read as "with careful steps" or "in chains" — the text is difficult; Targum has "in chains," LXX "trembling"). The MKT chooses "hesitantly." Agag's words ʾāḵēn sār mar hammāwet — "surely the bitter agony of death has passed" — are ambiguous: he may believe Samuel intends to spare him (ironic confidence), or he may be expressing relief that his captivity spared him death in battle. Either reading makes his execution in the next verse more abrupt.
33: šikkəlāh nashîm ḥarbaḵā — "your sword has made women childless (šikkēl, H7921)." Samuel's pronouncement is lex talionis applied to royal scale: the verb šikkēl (to bereave of children) frames the execution as proportional justice, not arbitrary violence. wayəšassēp šəmûʾēl ʾet ʾăḡāḡ — "Samuel cut Agag down" (šāsap, H8158 — a hapax; probably "to hew to pieces"). The act is described as happening "before YHWH" (lipnê YHWH) at Gilgal — it is a ritual completion of the ḥērem, not a judicial execution.
34: The parting of ways: Samuel returns to Ramah; Saul to Gibeah. The geography underscores permanent separation — Samuel never comes to Gibeah (Saul's base), and Saul never comes to Ramah (Samuel's home). The covenant mediator and the failed king now exist in entirely separate spheres.
35: nāḥam (third occurrence) — wəNIHYAH niḥam YHWH kî himliḵ ʾet šāʾûl ʿal yiśrāʾēl — "The LORD regretted that he had made Saul king over Israel." The verb closes the chapter as it opened it (v11). Samuel's grief (ʾābal, to mourn) mirrors YHWH's nāḥam; prophet and God share the same emotional response to Saul's failure. The chapter has moved from appointment (v1) to irreversible rejection (v35), and the bookend of nāḥam holds the arc together.
